turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kennedy has the opinion of the Court in case 17-21, Lozman v. City of Riviera Beach.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: This case involves the intersection of two sets of general principles, principles that define when arrests are lawful and principles that prohibit the government from retaliating against a person for having exercised the right to free speech. Petitioner Fane Lozman became a resident of the City of Riviera Beach, Florida in 2006 and from the start, Lozman was an outspoken critic of city officials and policies. He often expressed these views during City Council meetings. He often expressed those views during City Council meetings and he also filed a lawsuit alleging that the City Council had violated State Open Meeting Laws. In June of 2006, the City Council held a closed door session in part to discuss Lozman's open meeting lawsuit. According to Lozman, the transcript of that meeting shows the City Council members devised a plan to intimidate him based on his prior speech and for the lawsuit that he filed. Five months later, the City Council held a public meeting. Lozman stepped up to the podium during the public comment portion of the meeting. He began to discuss the recent arrest of an official from another jurisdiction. A councilmember interrupted Lozman and direct him to stop making those remarks. When Lozman refused, the councilmember told the police officer in attendance to carry him out of the room. The officer handcuffed Lozman and escorted him from the meeting. Lozman later filed this lawsuit against the city under 42 U.S.C. §1983. He alleged a number of incidents that under his theory showed the city's purpose to harass him in different ways including the arrest of the City Council meeting. Lozman claimed that the arrest was to retaliate against him for his protected speech. The jury returned a verdict for the city on all of the claims. Now, this case involves only Lozman's retaliatory arrest claim. On appeal, the Court of Appeals for the Eleventh Circuit determined that the jury necessarily found that the arrest was supported by probable cause and it held that the presence of probable cause defeated Lozman's First Amendment retaliatory arrest claim. The existence of probable cause to arrest on the part of the arrest and arresting officer has been conceded by Lozman in this case. And so the issue the parties deemed central to the case is whether the probable cause for an arrest bars a suit alleging that the arrest was to retaliate for speech protected by the First Amendment. That issue has divided the Courts of Appeals. But its resolution must await a different case that is because Lozman's claim is far afield from the typical retaliatory arrest claim. Here, Lozman does not sue the officer who made the arrest instead Lozman alleges that the city itself retaliated against him pursuant to an official municipal policy. In particular, Lozman alleges that the city through the council members, the legislators formed a premeditated plan to intimidate him based on his protected speech and then executed that plan through the same high officers by ordering his arrest at the City Council meeting. The fact that Lozman must prove the existence and enforcement of an official municipal policy motivated by retaliation separates Lozman's claim from the typical retaliatory arrest claim. An official retaliatory policy is a particularly troubling and potent form of retaliation. For a policy can be long-term, pervasive and difficult to dislodge. This is not to say that Lozman is ultimately entitled to relief or even a new trial. On remand, the Court of Appeals applying the relevant precedents may wish to consider any arguments in support of the District Court judgment that have been preserved by the city. Among other matters, the Court of Appeals may wish to consider one, whether a reasonable juror could find that the city actually formed a retaliatory policy to intimidate Lozman during the June 2006 closed door session. Two, whether any reasonable juror could find that Lozman's arrest constituted an official act by the city. Three, whether the city has proved that it would have arrested Lozman regardless of any retaliatory animus, for instance, if Lozman's conduct during prior City Council meetings had also violated valid rules as to proper subject of discussion thus explaining his arrest here. For these and other reasons set out in the opinion, the judgment of the Court of Appeals is vacated and the case is remanded for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. Justice Thomas has filed a dissenting opinion.
